speaker: interviewer
text: This tape number nine in an interview with Mrs. Hanna Lifschutz. Hanna, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right hand side is my mother, and the picture is when she was very young, probably in her 20s. And she died when she was 28. And this is my father, on the left hand side, who was her husband, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- his name is-- was Yossef. Yossef Rubin, and he was probably here-- he looks older, but he-- he must have been in his early 40s. He looks-- he looks ancient here, doesn't he? But he must have been-- the nose like my father. This was my mother. Look, she was lovely, wasn't she?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? CREW: ?] How old was she when she had you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She must have been 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture represents my grand-- my maternal grandparents, my mother's parents. That-- on the right hand side is my grandmother. And name was-- you know that I forgot her name? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know my grandfather's name was Abraham, but I don't remember-- And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kahane.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kahane.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kahane, yes, Kahane.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were also from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were from Poland, and they-- they were born in Portland and they-- they lived in Poland all their lives. I mean-- my grandfather died. He must have been 64. And my grandmother was killed in the-- in the-- in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holocaust. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the right hand side--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So should I say it, again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. You signalled me. OK, who's in these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're my two grandparents. My mother's father is on the left hand side, Abraham Kahane. And on the right hand side, my father's father. His name is Lazar Rubin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Different ages, of course. He was-- see how good-looking [INAUDIBLE]. Grandpa Kahane. Both of them are very handsome, I must say. How'd they know it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK, Hanna, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right hand side is my stepbrother, Rudolf. Rudolf Lerner was his name. Rudolf Lerner. What a good looking boy he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And you-- you told his story--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you the story--

speaker: interviewer
text: During the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you story--

speaker: interviewer
text: About--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then how he got ill in the-- in the war, and then he died and how I buried him, unfortunately, probably at the age of 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is my younger brother, half-brother, Michael, and we called him [? Micash. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rubin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rubin. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he must have perished when he was about 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was taken? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea what happened. I have no idea. Probably in one of the selections. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my stepmother, Rosa. She's the mother of the two boys, her-- Rudolf with her-- of her first marriage, and [? Micash ?] with Father. My father was-- is-- she-- he was his father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed with her a long time in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after-- I was altogether from the age of six until the end of the liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They must-- I have an album I must show you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, in this picture? What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- This is I, I, at the age of nine, I think. And I kept visiting my grandparents where I spent my first years from practically one year to six, in [INAUDIBLE]. On a visit, on a holiday visit. Look at my face. Who do I look like?

speaker: interviewer
text: This was, of course, before the war. Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Age of nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK. Who-- who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- this picture was given to me as a special present from my cousin, Selena Sokolow, in her elder age. On the-- on the-- on the right hand side, sitting, is Nahum Sokolow, who you know was very-- took part in the Balfour Declaration. And on the right hand side is his son, Florian, and standing is Selena Sokolow. And when we came from India, we-- we renewed our friendship, because I didn't know them in Poland at all. And we became very, very great friends till their deaths. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Selena?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selena was the daughter of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Daughter of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Florian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Florian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she gave me this photograph to be remembered, and I have very fond memories of quite a remarkable family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK. This is correction for the former explanation, and if you can tell us now, who's in this picture and what's the relation to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right hand side, sitting, is Nahum Sokolow. He was my father's uncle. His wife and my grandmother were sisters. Now, Nahum Sokolow, two children are with him. On the left hand side is Florian, and standing above them is Selena. And this picture was given to me at sometime in the-- in the early-- in the early '70s in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, in the middle, Pola Lewin. She-- this photograph was taken in still good days, just-- I think, just, just, just before the war. And these two postcards, I put them here-- she give it to me at some time-- some time ago-- I put it in particular, because they state the address in Kobe, in Kobe, Japan, where she went when she-- when she came to because coast of this-- of this wonderful rescue of the [INAUDIBLE]. And also, it has the German Nazi stamp. That's why I preserved the letters. And in this particular postcard, she-- we thank her all for the parcel of food she sent us from Japan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This on the-- on the-- this a-- a picture of Irena Kierszniewska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the one, with her husband on the right hand side, Roman, who was this very kind and noble Christian people, who accepted me in the time, in the-- on the Christian side when I was in desperate need of shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Roman, her husband, and they were in early 30s, at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And he was hiding you also afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- yes, he was helping me all the time. First the eight months, and then a few months more when I came in overnight to the garage to sleep overnight. And this the son, Andrew, who is see now 54 years old, and he was four years old when I left them. And I met them now, the whole family. I will show you the picture, maybe [INAUDIBLE]. The whole family in-- with [INAUDIBLE] and Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he has two daughters, married and two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still lives in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Not in [INAUDIBLE]. In Warsaw. And they all call me auntie. And when they write the letters, when the father wrote letters to me, and they continue, in every letter is a dry beautiful flower. I have collection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hanna, what's this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document which I got now in Poland. They never sent to me. It's a copy-- it's a photocopy of a diploma, which they-- which-- which honors the righteous Gentiles, for helping Jewish people during the war. The story of their-- of my survival and their help, was recorded in Yad Vashem, I think it must have been the first time in '58. And then in '78, I met a very charming gentleman in Yad Vashem, and I secured for them a-- a small pension, which [? hires-- ?] which [INAUDIBLE] from America. Sent them, and supposedly they still receiving. Apart from my constant and uninterrupted help, from 1946 since my arrival in India, till present day. They are, of course, very noble people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my friends I mentioned during our talk. On the right hand side is my friend who went to the same school in one year higher. She was just like a year older. And this is her first child. This is after we all were saved from Bobrowniki. She went with her husband on the top, Stefan, to displaced persons camp to Germany, and then to America. And I spent with them quite a long time, as I told you, in Poland, in Warsaw, in Bobrowniki. And this is Stefan, and he was the one--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: --conducting-- conducted the whole set up. It was a boyfriend to all his cousins, and allowed me to join them. And I thought-- he-- he's actually-- they live in Arizona. They have-- one child died. They have a son, doctor of chemistry, and Stefan is now 90 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: 92 years old, and we telephoned the other day. This is her sister, Janina.

speaker: interviewer
text: That joined you later on in Bobrowniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what's this picture about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is a picture-- this is a photograph I got from a friend of my husband, actually, and this is with his group of friends in Lithuania, in Kovno, I believe, in 1940-- 1940. Yes, in 1940, late 1940. And here on this very extreme left is my husband, Simon Lifschutz. My future husband, Simon Lifschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940, I said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Hanna, what's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is a picture which was also given to me by my sister. It was, presumably, 1941, when she was with a group-- a group of friends and-- who were refugees in Lithuania, just before sailing-- going through the Trans-Siberian Train to Vladivostok, and then going to Japan.

speaker: interviewer
text: She is right in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's right in the middle here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, Hanna, who's in picture here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the photograph I have probably taken with me when I-- This is looked like when I left-- when I left Poland in May 1946, on my way to India.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Well, what age you were? You were 20--

speaker: interviewee
text: What age I were. This is difficult, because when I arrived in India, my sister wanted to take two years of my life away, straight away. She was not sure, two or three, but we stopped her.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been 20-- you know, 19-- I must have been 24. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Who's in these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is how-- a few months after my arrival in Bombay. On the right hand side is my sister from few previous years. Not many. I think it was in '43. And my picture, on the left hand side, is from '46, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the wedding here is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was my wedding photograph in front of the Ritz Hotel in Bombay, India, 10th of October, 1947. And as you notice, I have got here not a little bouquet but a-- a whole broom.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Enormous. I don't know who provided it, but it's in there. But may-- may I add that I made the dress myself, and the headgear myself, everything myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 10th or the 16th?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- no, actually, 16th, you are right. 16th, 10, 1947. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, what's-- who is in this picture, Hanna? You see, in this picture is my husband, Simon, with a retinue of his chief-- some-- some-- some of his chief officers of workers, engineers, and, and glass technologists and-- and myself and the children, and his secretary Mrs. Marta [? Wartenberg, ?] of the left hand-- on the left hand side. And when-- on one of his arrivals from-- from-- from England, probably, where he purchased machinery for-- for the glass factory. And this was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Bombay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bombay.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Alexi, darling Alexi who was very small, and that is Vivian.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is myself. Vivian is on-- on-- on one side, and Alexi is on the other. And this is the real Alexi. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's myself, in the old, good time. And Vivian had to go to orthodontist, as you can see. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Alexi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in India?

speaker: interviewee
text: In India, Bombay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Hanna, what's this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very, very touching and a very special, unique document. You see, my husband, when he first started his career in Poland, as a glass expert in-- an expert in building-- in building glass factories, his-- his fortune, [INAUDIBLE], of course, took him to-- to India. First Burma, then India, then Hyderabad State, which belonged to the famous Maharaja of Hyderabad, Nizam. Very famous in his time. Afterwards, during the partition, Hyderabad belonged to India. Now, he had that this first work what he-- was actually his second job. He was employed by-- by the relative of the Maharajah of Hyderabad, and he built a vast factory. Now, he had unskilled-- about 1,000 [? unskilled ?] workers. Now, the workers, when they finished the job in Hyderabad, most of them came over with their families to Bombay to join him. Every time he built another factory, they joined him. And he was-- he loved them. He was kind to them. He was good to them. Sometimes he was very angry with them, if they didn't listen. But they were very grateful to him, because he taught them a proper skill to earn the living, took care of the children. He was really quite remarkable, my husband. And this-- in 19-- we were-- [INAUDIBLE] I came back with the children in '58 and '59, and in '62 my husband finished building the last factory, and he came to London. And in-- on the-- I don't know what is the date, '73. The engine-- the factory was-- the last factory was sold to the Maharaja or Maharana of Indo. And the workers and the glass technologists and the engineers and the retinue of all the different skilled technical mechanics, and people who could-- simple people who just helped with the enormous glassware. It was also about 1,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- in gratitude, they-- they sent-- I would like to read it. I can't see it, but could I read it somehow? Yeah. And they send this. It is written, 'Workers in India whose lives you have changed and trained us as a skilled workers and made us useful to ourselves, to our families-- and to our families. In gratitude and reverence, we are sending you our kindly to you, and our kindly mem sahib-- mem sahib means me-- your daughter, your son-in-law-- Vivian's husband-- and your Chottasahib-- he was the little one. Chotta means little. Little sahib. Our prayers and healthy New Year greetings.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And they signed. And some of them who couldn't sign put their fingerprint, and there were-- there were many, but the engineer said, 52 is enough. So 52 have signed, and 50-- some of them put fingertips-- and finger-- finger--

speaker: interviewer
text: Print.

speaker: interviewee
text: Prints. And-- and as you read it, some of the helpers, some of the fire-- the furnace for-- for maintaining the temperature of the furnace, and foremen, and foremen, and all kinds of people who came. And there is a beautiful story attached to this, which I don't know if you want me to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two workers, whose father lost, in the country, they lived in the-- they lost two bullocks, bullocks, you know, two two oxen, two oxen, a male and female. And they were-- they were-- they-- they died, and their father said, could your sahib help us? So the two-- and if-- if they help-- if you help us, my son will forever be in your-- not only gratitude, but-- but-- but like in your-- will be in your life and do whatever you want them to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and so my husband, of course, helped them to buy two new oxen, because you must have two, and the father was happy. And then when the factory was sold, one day we received a letter, through the old man, to his sons. Would my husband release the pledge? Could they work for somebody else? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the pledge was given that all my husband's life, they have to do what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Work for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what he wants to. So of course, we released them. Very touching story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very touching. OK, Hanna, can you tell me about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You see on the-- on the-- on the left hand side is the president of Israel, Ben-Zvi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second. Second president of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Second president after Weizmann, is Ben-Zvi. In the middle is my husband, and on the right hand side is the president [? aid ?] [INAUDIBLE], Yossi Karmel, with whom we are friendly all our lives. And we-- at the earlier stage of our lives-- lifes in India, Yossi Karmel was the first one to come from India with his young wife to-- to enlist funds for Israel. And it was very difficult for him, not knowing the community. And they were so different, so many different communities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we helped Yossi Karmel with the whole fund raising mission, and this took place a-- a few times in India, and then we continued all the time when we came to Israel. It was for United. [INAUDIBLE] was for Hebrew University. It was for Tel Aviv University. It was for-- for-- for every possible-- all, every possible-- every possibility. And we were-- we were delighted to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Yossi Karmel, who got very friendly with us and was this aid [INAUDIBLE] to the president, insisted taht we should meet-- that-- that the president will receive us. We didn't want, because my husband was a-- he was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Humble.

speaker: interviewee
text: --humble man, and he said, because we give money, we did-- we do help and give money because Israel-- we need Israel. We-- we can't live without Israel. This is our country, and this is-- this is our future. But-- but it was-- it is naturally an enormous honor, and after persuasion, we came over-- I was also there, but this is the picture which very kindly we were given the audience by President Ben-Zvi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Can you tell me about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were already living, of course-- my husband left India when we were living in-- in London, in Wimbledon-- and when we were in India, we were introduced by the first Israeli consul, Mr. and Mrs. [INAUDIBLE], to Mr. Yigal Allon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is on the horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- from the-- from the-- straightaway from meeting him, he very much liked my husband. He took an immediate liking to him, and they became quite friendly. And on a visit to Israel-- I think it must have been later, '67-- '66. I don't quite remember, '67, or '70, even, I don't-- my husband went to Israel and went to Ginosar, to the kibbutz there, and then that picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know about Mr. Allon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Mr. Allon was at one time a foreign minister of Israel, and he was one, I think, who proposed the plan of-- of-- of getting of getting-- of granting the Palestinians some autonomy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? Oh, this is a photo of, later, of President Katzir. I don't remember which year was it, but do you know? President Katzir? But it was-- The fourth president of Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fourth president of Israel. It was again-- because Yossi Karmel, who originally was this aide [INAUDIBLE] to the President Ben-Zvi, left his job, with the-- I think with the death of the president, and he became extremely active, and became one of the governors later. But was extremely active for years and years with the Tel Aviv University. And we, at one point, because of our work for Israel and some donations, we were given a laboratory in the Tel Aviv University. The laboratory-- I don't remember what sort of laboratory it was connected with-- with, but we-- it was-- it was-- it was called Hanna and Simon Lifschutz. And then he insisted again that we meet president Katzir. And on this-- on this occassion we met him. Must have been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '60-- '62. Yeah.
